extracted_rules.resource: Right to hold high office and state power
proof: [§15] For they find fault with the character of the popular orators and yet put them at the head of affairs and invest them with power over the whole state;
extracted_rules.reasoning: Isocrates notes that despite public criticism of their character, orators are systematically granted leadership roles and supreme power over the Athenian state.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: While orators are a specific professional class, they represent the primary political leadership in Athens, affecting the entire citizenry.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to public recognition and legal standing
proof: [§10] And those who are not endowed with these are condemned to go about in greater obscurity so far as public recognition is concerned than those who owe money to the state; for the latter have still the hope of paying off the fine assessed against them, whereas the former can never change their nature.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text explicitly compares the lack of public recognition for those with weak voices to the 'obscurity' and loss of status visited upon those who owe money to the treasury (atipia), though noting the latter is reversible.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to a specific legal category of citizens who have incurred fines and thus lost certain civic rights.

extracted_rules.resource: Retention of private wealth
proof: [§12] ...while I and mine not only abstain more than all others from the public funds but expend more than we can afford from our private means on the needs of the commonwealth;
extracted_rules.reasoning: Isocrates refers to the system of liturgies where wealthy individuals were expected or required to spend their private fortunes on public needs (the commonwealth).
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to the upper economic tier of the Athenian citizenry.

extracted_rules.resource: Socially approved access to higher studies
proof: [§26] ...I even commend that which has been set up in our own day—I mean geometry, astronomy, and the so-called eristic dialogues, which our young men delight in more than they should, although among the older men not one would not declare them insufferable.
[§28] but for those who are older and for those who have been admitted to man’s estate I assert that these disciplines are no longer suitable.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Isocrates describes a social norm where certain abstract disciplines (geometry, eristics) are permitted for youths as a deterrent from harm, but are deemed socially 'unsuitable' and 'insufferable' for those of man's estate or older.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This divides the male citizen population into two broad categories based on life stage.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to control and profit from own intellectual work
proof: [§16] ...where, I say, could you find men more reprehensible than these, who are not able to put before their students even a fraction of what I have set forth in my teaching but use my discourses as models and make their living from so doing, and yet are so far from being grateful to me on this account that they are not even willing to let me alone but are always saying disparaging things about me?
extracted_rules.reasoning: Isocrates laments that other sophists can use his written discourses as models for their own teaching and make a living from them without his consent or compensation, indicating a lack of legal protection for intellectual property.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This affects a small, highly specialized group of professional educators and writers.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to land and means of subsistence
proof: [§43] First they took the islands of the Cyclades... and, having driven out the latter, refrained from appropriating the lands of these islands for themselves, but instead settled upon them those of the Hellenes who were most lacking in means of subsistence.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Isocrates describes an Athenian policy of settling landless or poor Greeks on conquered islands rather than keeping the land for the state or the wealthy, effectively a state-sponsored resource redistribution.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to a broad class of Greeks (the poor/landless) across various city-states under Athenian influence.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to possess territory and cities
proof: [§46] ...having learned from the actual course of events that while according to law states and territories are deemed to belong to those who have duly and lawfully acquired them, in fact, however, they fall into the hands of those who are most practised in the art of warfare and are able to conquer their enemies in battle
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes the Spartan principle (and a general 'fact' of the time) that military superiority creates a de facto right to territory, overriding traditional legal ownership.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule describes a fundamental mechanism of interstate relations affecting all populations in the Peloponnese.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to govern subject states
proof: [§54] The Lacedaemonians, on the other hand, set up in their subject states a polity which resembled neither that which obtained among themselves nor those which have existed anywhere else in the world; nay, they vested in ten men alone the government of each of the states
extracted_rules.reasoning: Isocrates explicitly mentions the Spartan practice of establishing 'Decarchies' (councils of ten) to rule subject cities, centralizing all political power in a tiny, specific group.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: The rule concerns a very small, elite group of ten individuals per city-state.

extracted_rules.resource: Freedom from subjection
proof: [§53] Now after these events it came about that each of these cities in turn gained the empire of the sea—a power such that whichever state possesses it holds in subjection most of the states of Hellas.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The 'empire of the sea' (thalassocracy) is defined as a concrete mechanism that deprives other Greek states of their autonomy and subjects them to the hegemon.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This affects the majority of citizens within the numerous allied/subject states of the Greek world.

extracted_rules.resource: Freedom of movement at sea
proof: [§59] In the time of our supremacy, the barbarians were prevented from marching with an army beyond the Halys river and from sailing with their ships of war this side of Phaselis
extracted_rules.reasoning: Isocrates cites specific geographic limits (the Halys river and Phaselis) enforced by the Athenian hegemony to restrict the military and diplomatic movement of non-Greeks.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule applied to the entire Persian Empire (Barbarians) in relation to the Mediterranean.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to generous judgment in speech
proof: [§38] ...whereas if it turns out that I speak indifferently well, my hearers will make generous allowance for my years.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Isocrates identifies a social custom or 'rule' where advanced age permits a speaker to be judged less harshly for their performance in the public/intellectual arena.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies specifically to those of advanced age who are participating in public discourse.

extracted_rules.resource: Protection from execution without trial
proof: [§66] For example, in the present instance, if they bring up the fact that the law-suits of the allies were tried in Athens, is there anyone so slow of wit as not to find the ready retort that the Lacedaemonians have put to death without trial more of the Hellenes than have ever been brought to trial and judgement here since the founding of our city?
extracted_rules.reasoning: Isocrates contrasts Athenian legal procedure (trial) with the Spartan practice of executing Greeks without any judicial process, indicating that under Spartan hegemony, Greeks lacked protection from arbitrary execution.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule applies to the general population of Greeks ('Hellenes') living under Spartan influence or control.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to local adjudication of lawsuits
proof: [§63] ...to present in a false light the adjudication of lawsuits in Athens for the allies and her collection of tribute from them...

[§66] For example, in the present instance, if they bring up the fact that the law-suits of the allies were tried in Athens, is there anyone so slow of wit as not to find the ready retort that the Lacedaemonians have put to death without trial...
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text confirms that during the Athenian Empire, legal cases involving allied citizens were required to be tried in Athenian courts rather than in their home cities.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies specifically to the populations of the allied city-states within the Athenian maritime empire.

extracted_rules.resource: Retention of financial assets
proof: [§68] In the next place, they paid their quotas, not to preserve Athens, but to preserve their own democratic polity and their own freedom and to escape falling into such great misfortunes, through the setting up of oligarchies, as were suffered under the decarchies and the domination of the Lacedaemonians.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Allied states were required to pay tribute ('quotas') to Athens. While Isocrates justifies it as a means to protect their democracy, it constitutes a mandatory loss of local financial resources.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: The rule applies to the collective citizenry of the states allied with Athens.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to retain ancestral land and wealth
proof: [§70] ...whereas the Lacedaemonians laid waste the greatest cities of the Peloponnesus—states which in every way were eminent above the others— [§71] and now hold for themselves the wealth of those states...
extracted_rules.reasoning: Isocrates describes the Spartan practice of destroying major Peloponnesian cities and seizing their wealth and territory for Lacedaemonian use.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This affected the inhabitants of the major 'eminent' cities across the Peloponnesus conquered by Sparta.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to independent military command
proof: [§79] ...but even persuaded men of the rank of kings, who were accustomed to do in their own states whatsoever they pleased and to give orders to the world at large, to place themselves under his command, to follow him against whomsoever he might lead them, to obey his orders...
extracted_rules.reasoning: Isocrates notes that Agamemnon established a rule where even sovereign kings had to relinquish their absolute domestic power and follow the orders of a supreme commander in the pan-Hellenic expedition.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies only to the very small number of individuals holding royal rank.

extracted_rules.resource: Athenian citizenship and attached privileges
proof: [§94] our ancestors, on the other hand, gave the surviving Messenians a home in Naupactus and adopted the Plataeans who had escaped with their lives as Athenian citizens and shared with them all the privileges which they themselves enjoyed.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Isocrates highlights a historical legal precedent where Athens granted full citizenship rights to the survivors of Plataea, treating them as equals to native-born Athenians.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: The rule specifically concerns the survivors of a particular city (Plataea) rather than all foreigners generally.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to independence and internal management
proof: [§97] Now most people upbraid both cities because, while pretending that they risked the perils of war against the barbarians for the sake of the Hellenes, they did not in fact allow the various states to be independent and manage their own affairs in whatever way was expedient for each of them, but, on the contrary, divided them up, as if they had taken them captive in war, and reduced them all to slavery
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a systemic suppression of the political autonomy of smaller Greek states by the hegemonies of Athens and Sparta.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to a wide range of diverse city-states across the Hellenic world.

extracted_rules.resource: Protection from Persian rule
proof: [§106] for when the King had established them as masters over the Hellenes, they attempted to rob him of his kingdom and of all his good fortune, but when the King defeated them in battle on the sea and humbled them, they gave over to him, not a small contingent of the Hellenes, but all those who dwelt in Asia, explicitly writing into the treaty that he should do with these according to his pleasure
extracted_rules.reasoning: The Spartan treaty with Persia (the Peace of Antalcidas) explicitly stripped Greeks living in Asia of their protected status, handing them over to the absolute authority of the Persian King.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: It applies to a specific geographic subset of the Greek population.

extracted_rules.resource: Retention of local tax revenue and land
proof: [§116] when the Athenians should compel the Hellenes, to whom they had previously given lands and cities, to pay contributions and tribute to Athens in order that she might have the means to pay the kind of men whom I mentioned a moment ago.
extracted_rules.reasoning: As Athens transitioned to a maritime power, it established a rule of compulsory tribute (phoros) from its allies to fund its navy.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applied to all members of the Delian League/Athenian Empire.

extracted_rules.resource: Wages for military service
proof: [§116] a sea-power is not augmented by these but by the crafts which have to do with the building of ships and by men who are able to row them—men who have lost their own possessions and are accustomed to derive their livelihood from the possessions of others.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The shift to a maritime democracy created a systemic economic resource for the poor (the thetes), who received state pay for rowing in the fleet.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: It concerns the entire lower economic class of the citizen body.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to inherit the throne
proof: [§126] For Erichthonius, the son of Hephaestus and Earth, took over from Cecrops, who was without male descent, his house and kingdom; and beginning with this time all those who came after him—not a few in number—handed down their possessions and their powers to their sons until the reign of Theseus.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a specific period in Athenian history where political power and property were systematically passed down through male lineage within the royal house.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule applies to a very small, specific group of individuals (the direct male descendants of the king).

extracted_rules.resource: Right to advise the public
proof: [§140] and not even to suffer men to be heard who lend their own persons to base practices but deem themselves worthy to advise others how they should govern the state in order to advance in sobriety and well-being
[§141] on the contrary, they saw to it that each and everyone should look upon it as his duty to debar all such men from giving counsel to the public
extracted_rules.reasoning: Isocrates describes an ancestral Athenian rule where men who engaged in certain shameful occupations or personal behaviors (lending their persons to base practices) were legally or customarily barred from speaking in the assembly.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to a specific sub-section of the male citizen body identified by their lifestyle or occupation.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to advise the public
proof: [§140-141] or who have squandered what they inherited from their fathers on shameful pleasures but seek to repair their own fortunes from the public treasury... they saw to it that each and everyone should look upon it as his duty to debar all such men from giving counsel to the public
extracted_rules.reasoning: Citizens who had lost their inherited wealth through squandering were excluded from the right to address the public, as they were deemed to have a conflict of interest regarding the public treasury.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: It targets a specific economic class—those who have moved from 'wealthy' to 'poor' through mismanagement.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to be appointed to magistracies
proof: [§145] At the same time they appointed to the magistracies those who had been selected beforehand by the members of their respective tribes and townships
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text outlines a rule where local subgroups (tribes and demes) had the concrete right to select their own members for higher state offices.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This encompasses the majority of the citizen body participating in their respective local units.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to accept gratuities or public funds
proof: [§145] For the men who had been elected to office were required to neglect their own possessions and at the same time to abstain no less from the gratuities which are wont to be given to the offices than from the treasures of the gods.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Holding office in the 'ideal' past involved a mandatory loss of private economic gain and a prohibition on receiving gifts or public money.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies specifically to those chosen for public office, a significant but limited number of citizens.

extracted_rules.resource: Protection from severe punishment
proof: [§146] whereas those who were guilty of the slightest dereliction were involved in the deepest disgrace and the severest punishment.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Magistrates were held to a higher standard of legal accountability, losing common protections from severe punishment for even minor errors.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: Applies only to the subset of citizens currently holding magistracies.

extracted_rules.resource: Sovereign power to elect and audit officials
proof: [§147] and all men were agreed that no truer democracy could be found, nor one more stable or more beneficial to the multitude, than that which gave to the people at the same time exemption from such cares and sovereign power to fill the offices and bring to justice those who offended in them—exactly the position which is enjoyed also by the most fortunate among despots.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Isocrates describes the ancestral Athenian democracy where the 'multitude' (the citizen body) held the ultimate power to appoint magistrates and hold them legally accountable (euthynai).
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to the entire body of enfranchised citizens in the described polity.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to hold office based on election rather than lot
proof: [§153] I acknowledge that I am going to speak at length of the institutions of Sparta, not taking the view, however, that Lycurgus invented or conceived any of them, but that he imitated as well as he could the government of our ancestors, establishing among the Spartans a democracy tempered with aristocracy—even such as existed in Athens—, enacting that the offices be filled, not by lot, but by election
extracted_rules.reasoning: In the ancestral system Isocrates praises, offices were filled by election ('aristocracy') rather than the random lot, which restricted access to office to those deemed most capable by the voters.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: While all citizens vote, the pool of those eligible or likely to be elected is narrower than a lottery system.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to supervise public affairs through high councils
proof: [§154] ordaining that the election of the Elders, who were to supervise all public affairs, should be conducted with the very same care as, they say, our ancestors also exercised with regard to those who were to have seats in the Areopagus, and, furthermore, conferring upon the Elders the very same power which he knew that the Council of the Areopagus also had in Athens.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Both the Spartan Gerousia and the Athenian Areopagus (composed of former senior magistrates) granted specific supervisory powers to an older, elite demographic.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule applies only to a small subset of the population defined by age and previous high-office holding.

extracted_rules.resource: Land allocation and subsistence
proof: [§165] associating themselves with the people who were unable to gain a livelihood in their own states or who had fallen below the requirements of the laws—a class which is generally destructive to ordered states—, they urged these to take the field with them and to seek to improve the conditions of their present life

[§166] and when there proved to be many who were inclined and persuaded to take this course, they organized them into an army, conquered the peoples who occupied the islands of the barbarians and who dwelt along the coast of either continent, expelled them all, and settled in their stead those of the Hellenes who stood in greatest need of the necessities of life.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Isocrates describes a systematic policy where the city-state organized impoverished Greeks into military-colonial expeditions to seize land from 'barbarians' and provide them with a livelihood.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This targeted a specific socio-economic class (the landless or impoverished) within the Greek world.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to recover and bury war dead
proof: [§169] he failure to obtain a truce and was unable to recover the bodies of his dead for burial [...] implored the Athenians not to suffer such men to be deprived of sepulture nor to allow ancient custom and immemorial law to be set at naught—that ordinance which all men respect without fail

[§170] to advise her people that they be more reverent in their deliberations regarding the recovery of the dead [...] and to hint to them also that the Athenians would not countenance their transgression of the common law of all Hellas.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Isocrates references the 'common law of all Hellas' (an unwritten but enforced custom) that requires victors in battle to allow the defeated to recover their dead for burial.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This is a general rule governing inter-state relations and the treatment of all Hellenic soldiers.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to share in state privileges and honors
proof: [§178] [...] whereas the intelligent class among the Spartans held that such men were foolish in thinking that they could live in the same city with those against whom they had committed the greatest wrongs and yet govern the state in security; they themselves did nothing of the sort, but instead set up amongst their own class the only kind of equality and democracy which is possible if men are to be at all times in complete accord, while reducing the mass of the people to the condition of Perioeci, subjecting their spirits to a bondage no less abject than that endured by slaves.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Isocrates describes how the Spartan ruling class ('the intelligent class') systematically excluded the majority of the population from the 'equality and democracy' reserved for the elite, reducing them to the status of Perioeci.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule defines the primary social and political division of the entire Lacedaemonian state, affecting the vast majority of the population.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to own fertile land
proof: [§179] And having done this, they disposed of the land, of which by right every man should have had an equal share, seizing for themselves—the few—not only the richest but more than any of the Hellenes possess, while to the mass of the people they apportioned only enough of the poorest land so that by working laboriously they could hardly gain their daily bread.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text explicitly states that the Spartan elite seized the best land for themselves and left only small tracts of poor quality land for the 'mass of the people' (the Perioeci).
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to the specific sub-class of non-citizen inhabitants in the Lacedaemonian territory.

extracted_rules.resource: Protection from excessive military risk
proof: [§180] And, having despoiled them of all the rights which free men ought to share, they imposed upon them the greatest part in all dangers. For in the campaigns which were conducted by their kings they not only ranged them man for man side by side with themselves, but some they stationed in the first line, and whenever need arose to dispatch a relief-force anywhere and they themselves were afraid of the hardships or the dangers or the length of time involved, they sent them forth to take the brunt of the danger from all the rest.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Isocrates notes that the Spartans forced the Perioeci to take the most dangerous positions in battle and sent them on the most hazardous missions to spare Spartan lives.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This concerns the specific military obligations of the Perioeci class in Sparta.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to trial before execution
proof: [§181] For over these people, who have from the beginning suffered evils so dreadful, but in present emergencies are found so useful, the Ephors have the power to put to death without trial as many as they please, whereas in the other states of Hellas it is a crime against the gods to stain one’s hands with the blood of even the basest of slaves.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author contrasts Spartan law with the rest of Greece, noting that Spartan Ephors could legally execute Perioeci without any judicial process.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This is a specific legal power held by Spartan magistrates over a specific sub-group of the population.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to sanctuary and protection
proof: [§194] [...] with the object of seizing by force the sons of Heracles, who had taken refuge with us. However, he met with the fate which was his due. For so far did he fail of getting our suppliants into his power that, having been defeated in battle and taken captive by our people, he became the suppliant of those whom he had come to demand of us, and lost his own life.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Isocrates cites the historical/legendary precedent of Athens protecting the Heraclidae as a rule of the city to provide concrete protection and military defense to those who take refuge there.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: While mentioned in a specific historical context, it is presented as a fundamental principle of the Athenian state's character toward outsiders in need.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to instruction in letters
proof: [§209] For you will find that the latter have been both pupils and teachers of many discoveries, while the Lacedaemonians have fallen so far behind our common culture and learning that they do not even try to instruct themselves in letters—a science which has so much power that those who understand and use it become apprized not only of the things which have been accomplished in their own time but also of the things which have come to pass in any age whatsoever.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Isocrates explicitly states that the Spartans (Lacedaemonians) do not participate in the common Greek practice of literary education, characterizing them as being 'ignorant' of the science of letters which grants access to historical and contemporary knowledge.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to the entire male citizen body of a major Greek city-state (Sparta).

extracted_rules.resource: Protection from being compelled to steal
proof: [§211] For every day they send out their boys, from the very cradle, as it were, with such companions as each may prefer, ostensibly to hunt, but in reality to steal the property of the people who live in the country.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a systematic Spartan customs where young boys are compelled as part of their education to engage in theft against the rural population.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This refers to the specific age group of boys/youths within a specific civilization (Sparta).

extracted_rules.resource: Candidacy for important offices
proof: [§212] In this practice, those who are caught are punished with fines and blows, while those who have accomplished the greatest number of thefts and have been able to escape detection enjoy a higher esteem among their fellow-youths than the others, and when they attain to manhood, provided they remain true to the ways which they practised in youth, they are in line for the most important offices.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text outlines a specific path to high political office in Sparta based on the successful and undetected performance of seasonal thefts during youth.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This defines the specific entry criteria for the highest political offices within the Spartan state.

extracted_rules.resource: Protection from state-sanctioned theft
proof: [§211] For every day they send out their boys, from the very cradle, as it were, with such companions as each may prefer, ostensibly to hunt, but in reality to steal the property of the people who live in the country.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The description of the Spartan 'crypteia' or similar youth training implies that people living in the countryside were systematically subjected to the theft of their property by the youth of the ruling class as a matter of state policy.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: The rule affects a large segment of the population (those dwelling in the country/perioikoi or helots) in Spartan territory.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to use ambiguous arguments in court
proof: [§240] Seeking such an effect, you found without difficulty arguments of double meaning, which lend themselves no more to the purpose of those who praise than of those who blame, but are capable of being turned both ways and leave room for much disputation—arguments the employment of which, when one contends in court over contracts for his own advantage, is shameful and no slight token of depravity but, when one discourses on the nature of man and of things, is honorable and bespeaks a cultivated mind.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text identifies a social and quasi-legal norm where using 'double meanings' or deceptive sophistry in contract disputes for personal gain is categorized as 'shameful' and a sign of 'depravity,' effectively restricting the acceptable rhetorical resources available to litigants compared to philosophers.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies specifically to individuals involved in civil litigation and contract disputes, a significant but distinct subset of the citizenry.

extracted_rules.resource: Protection from unlawful exile and property seizure
proof: [§259] whereas in the city of the Spartans no one can show an instance of civil faction or slaughter or unlawful exile, nor of seizure of property or outrage to women and children, nor even of revolution or abolition of debts or redistribution of lands, nor of any other of the irreparable ills.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Isocrates highlights that Spartan society, unlike other Greek states, is characterized by a rule-bound stability where citizens are protected from the 'irreparable ills' of revolution, such as the seizure of property or unlawful exile.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to the entire body of Spartan citizens, distinguishing them from the citizens of other Hellenic states.

extracted_rules.resource: Protection from outrage and physical abuse
proof: [§259] whereas in the city of the Spartans no one can show an instance of civil faction... nor of seizure of property or outrage to women and children, nor even of revolution or abolition of debts or redistribution of lands, nor of any other of the irreparable ills.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text asserts a specific societal outcome/rule in Sparta where women and children are uniquely protected from 'outrage' (hubris/physical/sexual violation) that typically accompanies civil unrest in other Greek cities.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This relates to specific vulnerable sub-groups (women and children) within the Spartan state.

extracted_rules.resource: Protection from debt abolition and land redistribution
proof: [§259] whereas in the city of the Spartans no one can show an instance of civil faction... nor even of revolution or abolition of debts or redistribution of lands, nor of any other of the irreparable ills.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text identifies that in Sparta, the legal and social order prevents the 'abolition of debts' or 'redistribution of lands,' which provides a higher level of security for creditors and landholders compared to other Greek states.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to various economic actors, specifically those with land and those to whom debts are owed.

extracted_rules.resource: Freedom from foreign command
proof: [§257] for they, alone of the Hellenes, could say that, albeit so few in number, they had never followed the lead or done the bidding of any one of the populous states, but had throughout been free and independent
extracted_rules.reasoning: Isocrates notes a specific Spartan rule/status of refusing to submit to the leadership of other populous states, maintaining a unique level of military and political independence.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This defines the collective status of the Spartan citizen body relative to all other Greeks.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to address crowds at national festivals
proof: [§263] [...] but malign your discourses before the crowds at the national festivals, in which those who sleep outnumber those who listen; for these speakers hope that if only they can hoodwink such audiences their own compositions will rival yours in popular favour...
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a specific class of 'speakers' (rhetoricians/orators) who have the established right to address large assemblies at national festivals for the purpose of gaining popular favor and reputation.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to the professional class of orators and teachers of rhetoric, a significant but specialized group in Athenian public life.

extracted_rules.resource: Freedom from arduous intellectual or physical labor
proof: [§270] [...] to occupy myself with the completion of the discourse, at a time when I lacked but three years of having lived a century and when I was in a state of infirmity such that anyone else similarly afflicted, so far from undertaking to write a discourse of his own, would not even be willing to listen to one worked out and submitted by another.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Isocrates notes that at his extreme age (97 years) and state of infirmity, it is an established social expectation that one would not be capable of or required to undertake the intensive labor of composing or even reviewing complex works.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: While extreme old age is a universal human stage, the rule describes a broad social norm regarding the expectations placed on all elderly citizens.
